coredata.dc:title: Phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots loaded on TiO2 for enhanced photodegradation
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zengsheng
Wu, Hao
Li, Ming
Tang, Tao
Wen, Jianfeng
Li, Xinyu
coredata.dc:description: Reducing the direct recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs in a photocatalytic system is important to improve the photocatalytic efficiency. Transferring the photogenerated carriers to other adsorbates is a usual way to reduce the direct recombination probability. By synthesizing p-type phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots (P-GQDs) to form p-n junctions with n-type TiO2 nanocomposites, the photogenerated holes of TiO2 can be effectively transferred to P-GQDs, thus the direct carrier recombination of TiO2 is decreased, resulting in the conspicuous improvement of the Methyl Orange (MO) photodegradation efficiency to 95.5% in 14 min. This work provides a new type of p-n junction in highly efficient photodegradation system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorus-doping
Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalysis
Charge transfer
P-n junction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM image of P-GQDs, TEM images of (c) TiO2 and (d) P-GQDs-150/TiO2.
(a) XRD pattern of TiO2 and P-GQDs-150/TiO2, (b) FTIR spectra of P-GQDs, P-GQDs-150/TiO2 and TiO2, (c) Raman spectra of P-GQDs, P-GQDs-150/TiO2 and TiO2.
(a) XPS spectra of P-GQDs-150/TiO2 (upper) and TiO2 (lower), corresponding fine-scanned XPS spectra of (b) O 1s and (c) Ti 2p.

(a) UV–vis light absorption spectra of TiO2 and P-GQDs-150/TiO2. (b) Kubelka-Munk transformed functions of TiO2 and P-GQDs-150/TiO2. (c) PL spectra of TiO2, P-GQDs-100/TiO2, P-GQDs-150/TiO2 and P-GQDs-200/TiO2.
UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of (a) GQDs and (b) P-GQDs, Mott–Schottky curves measured versus SCE of (c) GQDs and (d) P-GQDs.

(a) Photocatalytic performance of different photocatalysts for removing MO under UV-light irradiation. (b) The corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetics data of different photocatalysts. (c) The recycle stability of P-GQDs-150/TiO2 for photodegradation of MO. C is the current concentration of MO and C0 is the initial one. (d) Schematic illustration for the light absorption of P-GQDs/TiO2 with various P-GQDs coverages.
Schematic diagram of possible reaction mechanism of GQDs/TiO2 and P-GQDs/TiO2 under UV light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots loaded on TiO2 for enhanced photodegradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science & Ministry-province Jointly-constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science & Ministry-province Jointly-constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science & Ministry-province Jointly-constructed Cultivation Base for State Key Laboratory of Processing for Mom-ferrous Metal and Featured Materials & Key Lab. of Nonferrous Materials and New Processing Technology, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reducing the direct recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs in a photocatalytic system is important to improve the photocatalytic efficiency. Transferring the photogenerated carriers to other adsorbates is a usual way to reduce the direct recombination probability. By synthesizing p-type phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots (P-GQDs) to form p-n junctions with n-type TiO2 nanocomposites, the photogenerated holes of TiO2 can be effectively transferred to P-GQDs, thus the direct carrier recombination of TiO2 is decreased, resulting in the conspicuous improvement of the Methyl Orange (MO) photodegradation efficiency to 95.5% in 14 min. This work provides a new type of p-n junction in highly efficient photodegradation system.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis technology is an excellent solution to deal with the water pollution because it is generally simple, convenient, efficient and environmentally friendly [1–3]. Among all the photocatalysts, TiO2 is the most commonly used due to its well-known characteristics such as usage convenience, high chemical stability and inexpensiveness [4–7]. However, the inefficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs of TiO2 is the main reason for its low photocatalytic activity [8,9]. To improve the photocatalytic efficiency, researchers have developed many methods, among which the heterojunction coupling catalysts based on TiO2 and semiconductor quantum dots is a competitive candidate to degrade organic pollutants [10,11], such as CdS, CdSe, PbS, etc [12–14]. Regrettably, these quantum dots are generally derived from toxic metals, limiting their practical application in photocatalytic degradation. Hence, it is necessary to find an environmentally-friendly semiconductor quantum dots.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel 0D carbon nanomaterial with lateral size less than 10 nm [15], advantages over traditional semiconductor quantum dots on simple synthesis, unique boundary effect, and the most importantly, biocompatibility [16,17]. It has exhibited extraordinary prospects in biosensors [18], biomedicines [19], solar photovoltaic devices [20] and catalysis [21,22], etc. Especially, GQDs, along with its heteroatom doping derivatives, have been demonstrated as an effective assistant to remarkably improve the photocatalytic activity [23–26]. For instance, Qu et al have reported that GQDs can distinctly improve the degredation ability of TiO2 on Methyl Orange (MO), due to the synergetic effect of absorption enhancement and efficient separation of photogenerated electrons and holes [27]. Li et al [8] and Luo et al [9] also reported that nitrogen- and sulphur-doped GQDs can effectively promote the degradation ability of TiO2 on MO. However, none of the above reports explained the semiconductor type of GQDs, and thus it is hard to clearly demonstrate how the electrons or holes transfer from TiO2 to GQDs. Dong et al [28], For the first time, showed that a new kind of heteroatom-doped quantum dots, namely, phosphorus-doped GQDs (P-GQDs) is a p-type semiconductor, thus, it can form a p-n junction with C3N4 to effectively facilitate the pollution degradation. Therefore, the decoration of P-GQDs on n-type TiO2 is also expected to form a p-n junction structure, through effective transfer of photo-generated carriers to lower the probability of their direct recombination, leaving charged particles sufficiently participate in the degradation process.

In our study, P-GQDs was synthesized by a facile hydrothermal stratagem using pyrene and Na2HPO4 · 12 H2O, and then loaded on the TiO2 surface to achieve P-GQDs/TiO2 p-n junctions. Photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed under ultraviolet (UV) light. The results indicated the electric fields generated by light excitation and formed by the p-n junction, energy level difference could effectively and rapidly transfer the photogenerated carriers, inhibiting the direct recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thus accelerate the pollutant degradation. This work provides a new perspective for the application of QDs and semiconductor composite photocatalyst.

Pyrene (C6H6 ≥ 98%, Aladdin), titanium dioxide (TiO2 ≥ 99.8%, Aladdin), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O ≥ 99%, Aladdin), absolute ethanol (C2H5OH ≥ 99.7%, Guilin Baer), sodium hydroxide (NaOH ≥ 96%, Guilin Baer) and nitric acid (HNO3 65–68%, Guilin Baer).

P-GQDs was prepared using a simple hydrothermal method [28]. 1 g pyrene was nitrated into 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in 80 ml HNO3 at 80 °C under refluxing and stirring for 12 h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the acid. Next, 40 mg 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and 600 mg Na2HPO4·12H2O were dispersed in an NaOH solution of ultrapure water (20 ml, 400 mM), following with an intensive ultrasonic (500 W, 40 kHz) treatment for 1 h. The suspension was then transferred to a 100 ml Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooled to room temperature, the obtained mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon products, and further dialysed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3,500 Da) for 24 h to remove sodium salt and unfused small molecules. Finally, the purified P-GQDs was freeze dried. GQDs was prepared with the similar conditions except the addition of Na2HPO4·12H2O.

P-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite were prepared using a simple hydrothermal method. First, 0.3 g TiO2 was dispersed in X ml P-GQDs aqueous solution (0.2 mg/ml, X = 100, 150, 200, respectively) with ultrasonication (500 W, 40 kHz) for 30 min. The suspension was then transferred to a 300 ml Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooled to room temperature, the sample was collected bycentrifugation and then washed with ultrapure water and ethanol for three times, and at last dried by freeze drying at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain the final product.The obtained sample was named as P-GQDs-X/TiO2 (X = 100, 150 and 200).

The morphologies, structures, chemical environments and optical properties of the samples were respectively performed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan), X-ray diffraction (XRD, MiniFlex-600, Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan), X-ray photoelectronic spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB-250XI, ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, NICOLET-6700 Hitachi High, Tokyo, Japan), UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 750 Shelton, CT, USA) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL, Edinburgh FL/FS900 Carry Eclipse, Cheadle, UK).

Photocatalytic measurements of the as-prepared catalysts were conducted by using methyl orange (MO)as the target organic pollutants. 50 mg photocatalyst was dissolved in 50 ml MO aqueous solution (10 mg/L) and treated by ultrasonication for 1 h in the dark to achieve adsorption and desorption equilibrium between the catalyst and MO. When the irradiation took place, 4 ml solution was taken out every 2 min from the reactor and filtered to remove the photocatalyst particles, and the concentration of MO was then determined by UV–visspectrometry(Lambda 950, PerkinElmer, USA) at 464 nm.

The electrochemical properties were measured on a CHI760E (Chenhua, Shanghai, China) electrochemical workstation using Na2SO4 (0.2 M)as electrolyte solution, with the prepared photocatalysts on nickel foam as the working electrode, the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and Pt wire as the counter electrode. The working electrode was prepared as follows: 9 mg sample and 1 mg polyvinylidene fluoride were well dispersed in a 10 ml utensil, and then the obtained catalyst was coated on a 0.8 cm × 0.8 cm nickel foam before drying at 70 °C for 6 h.

The morphology and microstructure of P-GQDs, TiO2 and P-GQDs-150/TiO2 were explored by TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) in Fig. 1 . P-GQDshas relatively uniform size distribution (Fig. 1a) and its plane lattice spacing was 0.243 nm (Fig. 1b), which corresponds to the (1120) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon in graphene [29]. As seen in Fig. 1c & d, TiO2 has spheroid shape, which could lead to a large specific surface and abundant reactive sites, and P-GQDs were well anchored on the TiO2 surface.

The phase structures of TiO2 and P-GQDs-150/TiO2 were exhibited by XRD. Shown in Fig. 2 a, six distinct diffraction peaks at 25.28°, 37.86°, 48.04°, 53.94°, 55.07° and 62.59° for TiO2 agree well with the standard, which corresponding to the (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5), (2 1 1) and (1 1 6) planes of TiO2 (anatase TiO2, PDF #99–0008). No obvious characteristic peaks attributed to P-GQDs were found, which may be due to the low loading content of P-GQDs. Namely, the P-GQDs modification has no significant influence on the structure of TiO2.

The surface groups of P-GQDs/TiO2 were analyzed by FTIR (Fig. 2b). The absorption peaks for pure TiO2 in the region of 440–800 cm−1 were ascribed to the classical stretching modes of Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C. Compared with TiO2, the absorption peaks for P-GQDs/TiO2 in the region of 950–1160 cm−1 were ascribed to the classical stretching modes of P-O. This result also demonstrated that P-GQDs was successfully coupled with TiO2. Broad absorption peaks from 3050 to 3540 cm−1 were caused by the existence of stretching modes of –OH.

The existence of P-GQDs in the composite was further verified by Raman spectroscopy. Shown in Fig. 2c, four distinct peaks at 144, 379, 515 and 640 cm−1 for pure TiO2, which corresponding to the Eg(1), B1g(1), A1g(1) + B1g(2) and Eg(3) vibration modes of anatase TiO2, respectively [30]. Two distinct peaks at 1350 and 1576 cm−1 for P-GQDs, which corresponding to the D and G bands of P-GQDs, respectively, assigned to sp3 defects and in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon in graphene, respectively [30]. Compared with TiO2, characteristic peaks of the P-GQDs can be vaguely identified since carbon quantum dots hardly keep its original crystal structure. The character Raman peak of TiO2 and uptrend at higher Raman shift in P-GQDs-150/TiO2 indicates that P-GQDs-150 has been successfully loaded on TiO2.

The surface chemical compositions of the TiO2 and P-GQDs-150/TiO2 were explored by XPS analyses. As surveyed in the fine-scanned XPS spectra (Fig. 3 a), they show strong signals of C 1s (284.62 eV), O 1s (529.84 eV) and Ti 2p (457.25 eV) peaks. The unexpected C 1s peak existed in TiO2 is a common experimental phenomenon when it was ever exposed to air. The P 2 s and P 2p peak signals only exist in P-GQDs-150/TiO2. These results also imply the surface modification of P-GQDs on TiO2. In Fig. 3b, the high-resolution O 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three different peaks at 532.7 eV (O–H), 531.5 eV (Ti–O–C) and 529.8 eV (Ti–O–Ti). As shown in Fig. 3c, the Ti 2p region was divided into two different peaks at 464.42 eV (Ti 2p1/2) and 458.67 eV (Ti 2p3/2). Taken together, these conclusions confirm that P-GQDs has been successfully modified onto TiO2.

The absorption spectra of the samples (Fig. 4 a)were used to study their optical properties. The pure TiO2 possesses an absorption edge at around 390 nm, which represented its bandgap of 3.2 eV (Fig. 4b). After incorporation of P-GQDs, the absorption edge of P-GQDs/TiO2 is barely shifted. The efficiency of photogenerated carrier recombination, separation and transfer is highly relative to the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra. The strong photocatalytic activity is generally relating to the high separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers. The PL spectrum of pure TiO2 exhibits typical emission peak at the wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 4c). Compared with pure TiO2, P-GQDs/TiO2 composite exhibits a considerable PL quenching, which indicates that the direct electron–hole recombination has been suppressed in P-GQDs/TiO2. We also can see that, the content ratio of P-GQDs and TiO2 has noteworthy effect on the carrier separation or transfer efficiency, thus affects the PL intensity. P-GQDs-150/TiO2 has the strongest PL quenching, so it is expected to have most carriers without recombination to participate in the degradation progress and thus has the highest degradation efficiency. The following experiments did confirm this prediction.

To make clear the band structure and semiconductor type of GQDs, UV–vis absorption spectra, PL and Mott-Schottky curve was conducted. As seen in Fig. 5 a & b, GQDs and P-GQDspossess an absorption edge at ~520 nm, meaning the optical band gaps of GQDs and P-GQDs are both ~2.35 eV. The PL spectra agree well with the absorption results. It is well known that the simplest way to test the semiconductor type is the Mott-Schottky curve. The curve of n-type semiconductors has a positive slope, while the p-type has a negative one. The Mott–Schottky curve examination is effective to determine the flat band potential (Efb) of a semiconductor. The Efb can be estimated by the intercept of the extrapolated straight line on the abscissa and considered as Fermi level. Assuming Fermi level is very close to the band edge, we take the obtained Efb as CB or VB edge. As shown in Fig. 5c & d, by using SCE as the reference electrode, the Mott-Schottky curves of GQDs and P-GQDs were obtained. The curve of GQDs presents a positive slope which means it is an n-type semiconductor, and on the contrary, the negative slope of P-GQDs indicates it is a p-type one. Via extrapolation, the intersections on the horizontal axis are − 1.15 eV and 0.96 eV for GQDs and P-GQDs, respectively. Because SCE has a deviation of 0.24 eV from the standard value (for example, versus standard hydrogen electrode, NHE), the CB edge of GQDs is evaluated to be − 0.91 eV and the VB edge of P-GQDs is 1.20 eV.

Methyl orange (MO) were usually used to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the catalysts under UV irradiation (Fig. 6 a). MO can be barely degraded without photocatalyst, and pure TiO2, P-GQDs and GQDscan photodegrade MO of ~ 62.6%, 56% and 69.9% in 14 min, respectively. When mixed GQDs with TiO2, one can see that n-type GQDs hardly synergize with TiO2, however, p-type P-GQDs co-work with TiO2 can degrade at least 89.28% MO under the same conditions. Especially for P-GQDs-150/TiO2, the removal rate exceeds 95.5%, approximately 1.53 times of the pure TiO2. From the above results, we inferred that the synergistic effect between TiO2 and P-GQDs resulted in the enhanced activities of P-GQDs/TiO2. To better understand the photocatalytic ability, the reaction kinetics was investigated by studying the degradation reaction rate constant k (Fig. 6b). Pure TiO2 has a low k (0.0691 min−1), and P-GQDs-150/TiO2 achieves a maximum of 0.2107 min−1, which is 3 times of pure TiO2 or 3.5 times of P-GQDs alone. The recycle stability is very important in catalyst application. Fig. 6c is the cyclic results of P-GQDs-150/TiO2. After successive four cycles, P-GQDs-150/TiO2 shows an extremely high stability and its catalytic ability is almost as same as the initial state. In a word, P-GQDs/TiO2 is a good photocatalyst which can degrade MO efficiently and quickly with high stability. We also noticed that, the photocatalytic efficiency of MO increased with increasing the quantity of P-GQDs from X = 100 to X = 150, however, further increasing P-GQDs loading (X = 200) the low loading percentage will result in the efficiency decrease. Fig. 6d explains why this phenomenon happens. When the P-GQDs coverage on TiO2 is low, the light is barely blocked by P-GQDs and almost completely absorbed by TiO2, the more P-GQDs can help transfer more photogenerated carriers in TiO2, thus the MO degradation is increased. However, when the P-GQDs coverage is too high to affect the light penetration to TiO2, not enough photogenerated carriers can be generated to result in decreasing of MO degradation efficiency, in spite of the fact that P-GQDs can help reduce the electron-hole direct recombination. Consequently, an optimum ratio between TiO2 and P-GQDs (here, X = 150 is the one which is closer to the optimum) is conducive to improve the photocatalytic activity.

Based on the above results, we proposed a possible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of the GQDs/TiO2 and P-GQDs/TiO2 composites under UV light irradiation (Fig. 7 ). The conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) edge potentials of semiconductor are calculated using the following equations:
(1)
E
CB
=
X
-
E
e
-
0.5
E
g
(2)
E VB
=
E g
+
E CB

In Eq. (1) and (2), X is the geometric average of the semiconductor atoms electronegativity. The X values for TiO2 are 5.81 eV (the Pearson absolute electronegativity of titanium and oxygen are 3.45 and 7.54 eV, respectively). Eg is the band gap energy. ECB and EVB are the CB and VB edge potentials of the semiconductor, respectively. Ee is the free electron energy (4.5 eV). The Eg value of TiO2 is 3.2 eV. One can calculate that the CB and VB of TiO2 are 0.29 and 2.91 eV, respectively (vs. the NHE at a PH of 7).

It is well-known that TiO2 is an n-type semiconductor able to create electron-hole pairs. From the results we discussed above (Fig. 5c), GQDs is a n-type semiconductor, so it forms n-n junction when adsorbed on the n-type TiO2. For GQDs, the CB and VB were more negative than that of TiO2. The photogenerated electrons could be transferred from the CB of GQDs to the CB of TiO2, while the photogenerated holes could be transferred from the VB of TiO2 to the VB of GQDs. Due to the energy level difference of GQDs and TiO2 is not large enough, the charge separation in GQDs/TiO2 was insufficient, which was responsible for its poor photocatalytic activity.

P-GQDs is a p-type semiconductor whose Fermi level is close to its valence band position and lower than the Fermi level of theTiO2. When P-GQDs is deposited onto the TiO2 facets, electrons diffuse from P-GQDs to TiO2 until the two Fermi levels are balanced. This causes the P-GQDs energy band to move upward and bend at the interface, resulting in the formation of a p-n junction and a built-in electric field E that points from TiO2 toward P-GQDs. When P-GQDs/TiO2 is excited by UV light, P-GQDs and TiO2 respond to UV light and produce photogenerated carriers. Electrons migrate from the valence band of P-GQDs and TiO2 to the conduction band, leaving holes in the valence band. Driven by the built-in electric field E formed by the p-n junction and energy level difference of P-GQDs and TiO2, the holes accumulated on the VB of TiO2 further migrate toward the VB of P-GQDs, while the photoinduced electrons in the CB of P-GQDs move to CB of TiO2. As a result, the electric field could effectively and rapidly transfer the photogenerated carriers, inhibiting the direct recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, leaving the free charges fully engaging in the oxidation process of the dye molecules, thus accelerate the pollutant degradation. As known, the photogenerated electrons on the CB can produce •O2 −, and the photogenerated holes on the VB oxidize water to produce OH radical, and these radicals are the key to degrade pollutants. Finally, the dye molecules are oxidized to CO2 and H2O by these active species.

In summary, we synthesized P-GQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst by facile hydrothermal methods. It exhibited efficient photocatalytic activity towards MO degradation under UV light irradiation. The electric fields generated by light excitation and formed by the p-n junction and energy level difference could effectively and rapidly transfer the photogenerated carriers, which greatly expedites the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and transfer of charges, thus obviously speeds up the pollutant degradation. In addition, this kind of catalyst also exhibits extraordinary recycle stability. It was found that the degradation efficiency of MO could be as high as 95.5% in 14 min. This work provides a novel TiO2-based p-n junction which has remarkable photocatalytic ability.
The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51662004, 11604061, 11664007, 11764011), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region of China (2018GXNSFAA050014, 2018GXNSFAA281191, 2018GXNSFAA050014), and Foundation of Guilin University of Technology (GLUTQD2002023).
